a last retrospect preface to 1850 edition i do not find it easy to get sufficiently far away from this book in the first sensation of have finish it to refer to it with the composure which this formal heading would seem to require
my interest in it be so recent and strong and my mind be so divide between pleasure and regret pleasure in the achievement of a long design regret in the separation from many companion that i be in danger of weary the reader whom i love with personal confidence and private emotion
besides which all that i could say of the story to any purpose i have endeavour to say in it
it would concern the reader little perhaps to know how sorrowfully the pen be lay down at the close of a two-year imaginative task or how an author feel as if he be dismiss some portion of himself into the shadowy world when a crowd of the creature of his brain be go from he for ever
yet i have nothing else to tell unless indeed i be to confess which might be of less moment still that no one can ever believe this narrative in the reading more than i have believe it in the writing
instead of look back therefore i will look forward
i cannot close this volume more agreeably to myself than with a hopeful glance towards the time when i shall again put forth my two green leaf once a month and with a faithful remembrance of the genial sun and shower that have fall on these leaf of david copperfield and make i happy
london october 1850
preface to the charles dickens edition i remarked in the original preface to this book that i do not find it easy to get sufficiently far away from it in the first sensation of have finish it to refer to it with the composure which this formal heading would seem to require

besides which all that i could have say of the story to any purpose i have endeavour to say in it
it would concern the reader little perhaps to know how sorrowfully the pen be lay down at the close of a two-year imaginative task or how an author feel as if he be dismiss some portion of himself into the shadowy world when a crowd of the creature of his brain be go from he for ever
yet i have nothing else to tell unless indeed i be to confess which might be of less moment still that no one can ever believe this narrative in the reading more than i believe it in the writing
so true be these avowal at the present day that i can now only take the reader into one confidence more
of all my book i like this the good
it will be easily believe that i be a fond parent to every child of my fancy and that no one can ever love that family as dearly as i love they
but like many fond parent i have in my heart of heart a favourite child
and his name be david copperfield
1869 the personal history and experience of david copperfield the younger chapter 1 i be bear whether i shall turn out to be the hero of my own life or whether that station will be hold by anybody else these page must show
to begin my life with the beginning of my life i record that i be bear as i have be inform and believe on a friday at twelve o clock at night
it be remark that the clock begin to strike and i begin to cry simultaneously
in consideration of the day and hour of my birth it be declare by the nurse and by some sage woman in the neighbourhood who have take a lively interest in i several month before there be any possibility of our become personally acquaint first that i be destine to be unlucky in life and secondly that i be privileged to see ghost and spirit both these gift inevitably attach as they believe to all unlucky infant of either gender bear towards the small hour on a friday night
i need say nothing here on the first head because nothing can show well than my history whether that prediction be verify or falsify by the result
on the second branch of the question i will only remark that unless i run through that part of my inheritance while i be still a baby i have not come into it yet
but i do not at all complain of have be keep out of this property and if anybody else should be in the present enjoyment of it he be heartily welcome to keep it
i be bear with a caul which be advertise for sale in the newspaper at the low price of fifteen guineas
whether sea-go people be short of money about that time or be short of faith and preferred cork jacket i donot know all i know be that there be but one solitary bidding and that be from an attorney connect with the bill-broke business who offer two pound in cash and the balance in sherry but decline to be guarantee from drown on any high bargain
consequently the advertisement be withdraw at a dead loss for as to sherry my poor dear mother s own sherry be in the market then and ten year afterwards the caul be put up in a raffle down in our part of the country to fifty member at half-a-crown a head the winner to spend five shilling
i be present myself and i remember to have feel quite uncomfortable and confused at a part of myself be dispose of in that way
the caul be win i recollect by an old lady with a hand-basket who very reluctantly produce from it the stipulate five shilling all in halfpence and twopence halfpenny short as it take an immense time and a great waste of arithmetic to endeavour without any effect to prove to she
it be a fact which will be long remember as remarkable down there that she be never drown but die triumphantly in bed at ninety-two
i have understand that it be to the last her proud boast that she never have be on the water in her life except upon a bridge and that over her tea to which she be extremely partial she to the last express her indignation at the impiety of mariner and other who have the presumption to go meandering about the world
it be in vain to represent to she that some convenience tea perhaps include result from this objectionable practice
she always return with great emphasis and with an instinctive knowledge of the strength of her objection let we have no meandering not to meander myself at present i will go back to my birth
i be bear at blunderstone in suffolk or there by as they say in scotland
i be a posthumous child
my father s eye have close upon the light of this world six month when mine open on it
there be something strange to i even now in the reflection that he never see i and something strange yet in the shadowy remembrance that i have of my first childish association with his white grave-stone in the churchyard and of the indefinable compassion i use to feel for it lie out alone there in the dark night when our little parlour be warm and bright with fire and candle and the door of our house be almost cruelly it seem to i sometimes bolt and lock against it
an aunt of my father s and consequently a great-aunt of mine of whom i shall have more to relate by and by be the principal magnate of our family
miss trotwood or miss betsey as my poor mother always call she when she sufficiently overcome her dread of this formidable personage to mention she at all which be seldom have be marry to a husband young than herself who be very handsome except in the sense of the homely adage handsome be that handsome do for he be strongly suspect of have beat miss betsey and even of have once on a dispute question of supply make some hasty but determined arrangement to throw she out of a two pair of stair window
these evidence of an incompatibility of temper induce miss betsey to pay he off and effect a separation by mutual consent
he go to india with his capital and there accord to a wild legend in our family he be once see rid on an elephant in company with a baboon but i think it must have be a baboo or a begum
anyhow from india tiding of his death reach home within ten year
how they affect my aunt nobody know for immediately upon the separation she take her maiden name again buy a cottage in a hamlet on the sea-coast a long way off establish herself there as a single woman with one servant and be understand to live seclude ever afterwards in an inflexible retirement
my father have once be a favourite of her i believe but she be mortally affront by his marriage on the ground that my mother be a wax doll
she have never see my mother but she know she to be not yet twenty
my father and miss betsey never meet again
he be double my mother s age when he marry and of but a delicate constitution
he die a year afterwards and as i have say six month before i come into the world
this be the state of matter on the afternoon of what i may be excuse for call that eventful and important friday
i can make no claim therefore to have know at that time how matter stand or to have any remembrance found on the evidence of my own sens of what follow
my mother be sit by the fire but poorly in health and very low in spirit look at it through her tear and despond heavily about herself and the fatherless little stranger who be already welcome by some gross of prophetic pin in a drawer upstairs to a world not at all excited on the subject of his arrival my mother i say be sit by the fire that bright windy march afternoon very timid and sad and very doubtful of ever come alive out of the trial that be before she when lift her eye as she dry they to the window opposite she see a strange lady come up the garden
my mother have a sure foreboding at the second glance that it be miss betsey
the set sun be glow on the strange lady over the garden-fence and she come walk up to the door with a fell rigidity of figure and composure of countenance that could have belong to nobody else
when she reach the house she give another proof of her identity
my father have often hint that she seldom conduct herself like any ordinary christian and now instead of ring the bell she come and look in at that identical window press the end of her nose against the glass to that extent that my poor dear mother use to say it become perfectly flat and white in a moment
she give my mother such a turn that i have always be convince i be indebted to miss betsey for have be bear on a friday

chapter 2 i observe the first object that assume a distinct presence before i as i look far back into the blank of my infancy be my mother with her pretty hair and youthful shape and peggotty with no shape at all and eye so dark that they seem to darken their whole neighbourhood in her face and cheek and arm so hard and red that i wonder the bird donot peck she in preference to apple
i believe i can remember these two at a little distance apart dwarf to my sight by stoop down or kneel on the floor and i go unsteadily from the one to the other
i have an impression on my mind which i cannot distinguish from actual remembrance of the touch of peggotty s forefinger as she use to hold it out to i and of its be roughen by needlework like a pocket nutmeg-grater
this may be fancy though i think the memory of most of we can go far back into such time than many of we suppose just as i believe the power of observation in number of very young child to be quite wonderful for its closeness and accuracy
indeed i think that most grown man who be remarkable in this respect may with great propriety be say not to have lose the faculty than to have acquire it the rather as i generally observe such man to retain a certain freshness and gentleness and capacity of be please which be also an inheritance they have preserve from their childhood
i might have a misgiving that i be meander in stop to say this but that it bring i to remark that i build these conclusion in part upon my own experience of myself and if it should appear from anything i may set down in this narrative that i be a child of close observation or that as a man i have a strong memory of my childhood i undoubtedly lie claim to both of these characteristic
look back as i be say into the blank of my infancy the first object i can remember as stand out by themselves from a confusion of thing be my mother and peggotty
what else do i remember
let i see
there come out of the cloud our house not new to i but quite familiar in its early remembrance
on the ground-floor be peggotty s kitchen open into a back yard with a pigeon-house on a pole in the centre without any pigeon in it a great dog- kennel in a corner without any dog and a quantity of fowl that look terribly tall to i walk about in a menacing and ferocious manner
there be one cock who get upon a post to crow and seem to take particular notice of i as i look at he through the kitchen window who make i shiver he be so fierce
of the geese outside the side-gate who come waddle after i with their long neck stretch out when i go that way i dream at night as a man environ by wild beast might dream of lion
here be a long passage what an enormous perspective i make of it
lead from peggotty s kitchen to the front door
a dark store-room open out of it and that be a place to be run past at night for i donot know what may be among those tub and jar and old tea-chest when there be nobody in there with a dimly-burn light let a mouldy air come out of the door in which there be the smell of soap pickle pepper candle and coffee all at one whiff
then there be the two parlour the parlour in which we sit of an evening my mother and i and peggotty for peggotty be quite our companion when her work be do and we be alone and the good parlour where we sit on a sunday grandly but not so comfortably
there be something of a doleful air about that room to i for peggotty have tell i i donot know when but apparently age ago about my father s funeral and the company have their black cloak put on
one sunday night my mother read to peggotty and i in there how lazarus be raise up from the dead
and i be so frightened that they be afterwards oblige to take i out of bed and show i the quiet churchyard out of the bedroom window with the dead all lie in their graf at rest below the solemn moon
there be nothing half so green that i know anywhere as the grass of that churchyard nothing half so shady as its tree nothing half so quiet as its tombstone
the sheep be feed there when i kneel up early in the morning in my little bed in a closet within my mother s room to look out at it and i see the red light shin on the sun-dial and think within myself be the sun-dial glad i wonder that it can tell the time again here be our pew in the church
what a high-back pew

but though peggotty s eye wander she be much offend if mine do and frown to i as i stand upon the seat that i be to look at the clergyman
but i cannot always look at he i know he without that white thing on and i be afraid of his wonder why i stare so and perhaps stop the service to inquire and what be i to do
itbe a dreadful thing to gape but i must do something

still nobody appear to claim the dusty youngster from blunderstone suffolk

here as i sit look at the parcel package and book and inhale the smell of stable ever since associate with that morning a procession of most tremendous consideration begin to march through my mind

murdstone have devise this plan to get rid of i what should i do

when he have put up his thing for the night he take out his flute and blow at it until i almost think he would gradually blow his whole be into the large hole at the top and ooze away at the key
i picture my small self in the dimly-light room sit with my head upon my hand listen to the doleful performance of mr
mell and con tomorrow s lesson
i picture myself with my book shut up still listen to the doleful performance of mr
mell and listen through it to what use to be at home and to the blowing of the wind on yarmouth flat and feel very sad and solitary
i picture myself go up to bed among the unused room and sit on my bed-side cry for a comfortable word from peggotty
i picture myself come downstairs in the morning and look through a long ghastly gash of a staircase window at the school-bell hang on the top of an out-house with a weathercock above it and dread the time when it shall ring j

creakle
i cannot think i be a very dangerous character in any of these aspect but in all of they i carry the same warning on my back

but he have these peculiarity and at first they frighten i though i soon get use to they

accordingly it be lock up in his box and draw off by himself in a phial and administer to i through a piece of quill in the cork when i be suppose to be in want of a restorative
sometimes to make it a more sovereign specific he be so kind as to squeeze orange juice into it or to stir it up with ginger or dissolve a peppermint drop in it and although i cannot assert that the flavour be improve by these experiment or that it be exactly the compound one would have choose for a stomachic the last thing at night and the first thing in the morning i drink it gratefully and be very sensible of his attention

in a school carry on by sheer cruelty whether it be preside over by a dunce or not there be not likely to be much learn
i believe our boy be generally as ignorant a set as any schoolboy in existence they be too much troubled and knock about to learn they could no more do that to advantage than any one can do anything to advantage in a life of constant misfortune torment and worry

the rest of the half-year be a jumble in my recollection of the daily strife and struggle of our life of the wan summer and the change season of the frosty morning when we be ring out of bed and the cold cold smell of the dark night when we be ring into bed again of the evening schoolroom dimly light and indifferently warm and the morning schoolroom which be nothing but a great shivering-machine of the alternation of boil beef with roast beef and boil mutton with roast mutton of clod of bread-and-butter dog s-eare lesson-book crack slate tear-blot copy-book caning rulering hair-cutting rainy sundays suet-pudding and a dirty atmosphere of ink surround all

i say yes and your shirt say miss murdstone have you bring them home yes ma am
i have bring home all my clothe this be all the consolation that her firmness administer to i
i do not doubt that she have a choice pleasure in exhibit what she call her self-command and her firmness and her strength of mind and her common sense and the whole diabolical catalogue of her unamiable quality on such an occasion
she be particularly proud of her turn for business and she show it now in reduce everything to pen and ink and be move by nothing
all the rest of that day and from morning to night afterwards she sit at that desk scratch composedly with a hard pen speak in the same imperturbable whisper to everybody never relax a muscle of her face or soften a tone of her voice or appear with an atom of her dress astray
her brother take a book sometimes but never read it that i see
he would open it and look at it as if he be read but would remain for a whole hour without turn the leaf and then put it down and walk to and fro in the room
i use to sit with fold hand watch he and count his footstep hour after hour
he very seldom speak to she and never to i
he seem to be the only restless thing except the clock in the whole motionless house
in these day before the funeral i see but little of peggotty except that in pass up or down stair i always find she close to the room where my mother and her baby lay and except that she come to i every night and sit by my bed s head while i go to sleep

oh no and hold her hand

chapter 11 i begin life on my own account and don t like it i know enough of the world now to have almost lose the capacity of be much surprised by anything but it be matter of some surprise to i even now that i can have be so easily throw away at such an age

murdstone and grinby s trade be among a good many kind of people but an important branch of it be the supply of wine and spirit to certain packet ship
i forget now where they chiefly go but i think there be some among they that make voyage both to the east and west indies
i know that a great many empty bottle be one of the consequence of this traffic and that certain man and boy be employ to examine they against the light and reject those that be flawed and to rinse and wash they

in this house and with this family i pass my leisure time

the pudding at that shop be make of currant and be rather a special pudding but be dear twopennyworth not be large than a pennyworth of more ordinary pudding
a good shop for the latter be in the strand somewhere in that part which have be rebuild since
it be a stout pale pudding heavy and flabby and with great flat raisin in it stick in whole at wide distance apart
it come up hot at about my time every day and many a day do i dine off it
when i din regularly and handsomely i have a saveloy and a penny loaf or a fourpenny plate of red beef from a cook s shop or a plate of bread and cheese and a glass of beer from a miserable old public-house opposite our place of business call the lion or the lion and something else that i have forget
once i remember carry my own bread which i have bring from home in the morning under my arm wrap in a piece of paper like a book and go to a famous alamode beef-house near drury lane and order a small plate of that delicacy to eat with it
what the waiter think of such a strange little apparition come in all alone i donot know but i can see he now star at i as i eat my dinner and bring up the other waiter to look
i give he a halfpenny for himself and i wish he havenot take it
we have half-an-hour i think for tea
when i have money enough i use to get half-a-pint of ready-make coffee and a slice of bread and butter
when i have none i use to look at a venison shop in fleet street or i have stroll at such a time as far as covent garden market and star at the pineapple
i be fond of wander about the adelphi because it be a mysterious place with those dark arch
i see myself emerge one evening from some of these arch on a little public-house close to the river with an open space before it where some coal-heaver be dance to look at whom i sit down upon a bench
i wonder what they think of i
i be such a child and so little that frequently when i go into the bar of a strange public-house for a glass of ale or porter to moisten what i have have for dinner they be afraid to give it i
i remember one hot evening i go into the bar of a public-house and say to the landlord what be your good your very well ale a glass for it be a special occasion
i donot know what
it may have be my birthday
twopence-halfpenny say the landlord be the price of the genuine stunning ale then say i produce the money just draw i a glass of the genuine stunning if you please with a good head to it the landlord look at i in return over the bar from head to foot with a strange smile on his face and instead of draw the beer look round the screen and say something to his wife
she come out from behind it with her work in her hand and join he in survey i
here we stand all three before i now
the landlord in his shirt-sleeve lean against the bar window-frame his wife look over the little half-door and i in some confusion look up at they from outside the partition
they ask i a good many question as what my name be how old i be where i live how i be employ and how i come there
to all of which that i might commit nobody i invent i be afraid appropriate answer
they serve i with the ale though i suspect it be not the genuine stunning and the landlord s wife open the little half-door of the bar and bend down give i my money back and give i a kiss that be half admiring and half compassionate but all womanly and good i be sure
i know i do not exaggerate unconsciously and unintentionally the scantiness of my resource or the difficulty of my life
i know that if a shilling be give i by mr
quinion at any time i spend it in a dinner or a tea
i know that i work from morning until night with common man and boy a shabby child
i know that i lounge about the street insufficiently and unsatisfactorily feed
i know that but for the mercy of god i might easily have be for any care that be take of i a little robber or a little vagabond
yet i hold some station at murdstone and grinby s too
besides that mr
quinion do what a careless man so occupy and deal with a thing so anomalous could to treat i as one upon a different footing from the rest i never say to man or boy how it be that i come to be there or give the least indication of be sorry that i be there
that i suffer in secret and that i suffer exquisitely no one ever know but i
how much i suffer it be as i have say already utterly beyond my power to tell
but i keep my own counsel and i do my work
i know from the first that if i could not do my work as well as any of the rest i could not hold myself above slight and contempt
i soon become at least as expeditious and as skilful as either of the other boy
though perfectly familiar with they my conduct and manner be different enough from theirs to place a space between we
they and the man generally speak of i as the little gent or the young suffolker a certain man name gregory who be foreman of the packer and another name tipp who be the carman and wear a red jacket use to address i sometimes as david but i think it be mostly when we be very confidential and when i have make some effort to entertain they over our work with some result of the old reading which be fast perish out of my remembrance

in my forlorn state i become quite attach to the family and use to walk about busy with mrs

on a saturday night which be my grand treat partly because it be a great thing to walk home with six or seven shilling in my pocket look into the shop and think what such a sum would buy and partly because i go home early mrs

chapter 17 somebody turn up it have not occur to i to mention peggotty since i run away but of course i write she a letter almost as soon as i be house at dover and another and a long letter contain all particular fully related when my aunt take i formally under her protection

how many a summer hour have i know to be but blissful minute to he in the cricket-field

he be an universal favourite and his ingenuity in little thing be transcendent

chapter 18 a retrospect my school-day

chapter 19 i look about me and make a discovery i be doubtful whether i be at heart glad or sorry when my school-day draw to an end and the time come for my leave doctor strong s

chapter 21 little em ly there be a servant in that house a man who i understand be usually with steerforth and have come into his service at the university who be in appearance a pattern of respectability
i believe there never exist in his station a more respectable-look man
he be taciturn soft-footed very quiet in his manner deferential observant always at hand when want and never near when not want but his great claim to consideration be his respectability
he have not a pliant face he have rather a stiff neck rather a tight smooth head with short hair cling to it at the side a soft way of speak with a peculiar habit of whisper the letter s so distinctly that he seem to use it oftener than any other man but every peculiarity that he have he make respectable
if his nose have be upside-down he would have make that respectable
he surround himself with an atmosphere of respectability and walk secure in it
it would have be next to impossible to suspect he of anything wrong he be so thoroughly respectable
nobody could have think of put he in a livery he be so highly respectable
to have impose any derogatory work upon he would have be to inflict a wanton insult on the feeling of a most respectable man
and of this i noticed- the woman-servant in the household be so intuitively conscious that they always do such work themselves and generally while he read the paper by the pantry fire
such a self-contain man i never see
but in that quality as in every other he possess he only seem to be the more respectable
even the fact that no one know his christian name seem to form a part of his respectability
nothing could be object against his surname littimer by which he be know

it be occasion i suppose by the reverend nature of respectability in the abstract but i feel particularly young in this man s presence

chapter 24 my first dissipation it be a wonderfully fine thing to have that lofty castle to myself and to feel when i shut my outer door like robinson crusoe when he have get into his fortification and pull his ladder up after he
it be a wonderfully fine thing to walk about town with the key of my house in my pocket and to know that i could ask any fellow to come home and make quite sure of its being inconvenient to nobody if it be not so to i

very good

chapter 40 the wanderer we have a very serious conversation in buckingham street that night about the domestic occurrence i have detail in the last chapter

dick and i sit quietly by the fire she keep pass in and out along this measured track at an unchanging pace with the regularity of a clock-pendulum

chapter 58 absence it be a long and gloomy night that gather on i haunt by the ghost of many hope of many dear remembrance many error many unavaile sorrow and regret
i go away from england not know even then how great the shock be that i have to bear
i leave all who be dear to i and go away and believe that i have bear it and it be past
as a man upon a field of battle will receive a mortal hurt and scarcely know that he be strike so i when i be leave alone with my undisciplined heart have no conception of the wound with which it have to strive
the knowledge come upon i not quickly but little by little and grain by grain
the desolate feeling with which i go abroad deepen and widen hourly
at first it be a heavy sense of loss and sorrow wherein i could distinguish little else
by imperceptible degree it become a hopeless consciousness of all that i have lose love friendship interest of all that have be shatter my first trust my first affection the whole airy castle of my life of all that remain a ruin blank and waste lie wide around i unbroken to the dark horizon
if my grief be selfish i do not know it to be so
i mourn for my child-wife take from her bloom world so young
i mourn for he who might have win the love and admiration of thousand as he have win mine long ago
i mourn for the break heart that have find rest in the stormy sea and for the wandering remnant of the simple home where i have hear the night-wind blowing when i be a child
from the accumulate sadness into which i fall i have at length no hope of ever issue again
i roam from place to place carry my burden with i everywhere
i feel its whole weight now and i droop beneath it and i say in my heart that it could never be lightened
when this despondency be at its bad i believe that i should die
sometimes i think that i would like to die at home and actually turn back on my road that i might get there soon
at other time i pass on far away from city to city seek i know not what and try to leave i know not what behind
it be not in my power to retrace one by one all the weary phase of distress of mind through which i pass
there be some dream that can only be imperfectly and vaguely describe and when i oblige myself to look back on this time of my life i seem to be recall such a dream
i see myself pass on among the novelty of foreign town palace cathedral temple picture castle tomb fantastic street the old abide place of history and fancy as a dreamer might bear my painful load through all and hardly conscious of the object as they fade before i
listlessness to everything but brood sorrow be the night that fall on my undisciplined heart
let i look up from it as at last i do thank heaven
and from its long sad wretched dream to dawn
for many month i travel with this ever-darken cloud upon my mind
some blind reason that i have for not return home reason then struggle within i vainly for more distinct expression keep i on my pilgrimage
sometimes i have proceed restlessly from place to place stop nowhere sometimes i have linger long in one spot
i have have no purpose no sustain soul within i anywhere
i be in switzerland
i have come out of italy over one of the great pass of the alps and have since wander with a guide among the by-way of the mountain
if those awful solitude have speak to my heart i do not know it
i have find sublimity and wonder in the dread height and precipice in the roar torrent and the waste of ice and snow but as yet they have teach i nothing else
i come one evening before sunset down into a valley where i be to rest
in the course of my descent to it by the wind track along the mountain-side from which i see it shin far below i think some long-unwonted sense of beauty and tranquillity some soften influence awaken by its peace move faintly in my breast
i remember pause once with a kind of sorrow that be not all oppressive not quite despairing

i come into the valley as the evening sun be shin on the remote height of snow that close it in like eternal cloud
the basis of the mountain form the gorge in which the little village lay be richly green and high above this gentler vegetation grow forest of dark fir cleave the wintry snow-drift wedge-like and stem the avalanche
above these be range upon range of craggy steep grey rock bright ice and smooth verdure-speck of pasture all gradually blend with the crowning snow

and the old name in the old tone fall so naturally on my ear

what with sheep-farming and what with stock-farming and what with one thing and what with t other we be as well to do as well could be

but theer be some poor folk aboard as have illness among them and she take care of they and theer be the child in our company and she take care of they and so she get to be busy and to be do good and that help she when do she first hear of it i ask
i kep it from her arter i heerd on t say mr
peggotty go on nigh a year
we be live then in a solitary place but among the beautifull tree and with the rose a-cover our beein to the roof
theer come along one day when i be out a-work on the land a traveller from our own norfolk or suffolk in england i doen t rightly mind which and of course we take he in and giv he to eat and drink and make he welcome
we all do that all the colony over
hewould get an old newspaper with he and some other account in print of the storm
thatbe how she know d it
when i come home at night i find she know d it he drop his voice as he say these word and the gravity i so well remember overspread his face

i doen t know

a slight figure say mr
peggotty look at the fire kiender worn soft sorrowful blue eye a delicate face a pritty head lean a little down a quiet voice and way timid a most

no one know how tis
she might have marry well a mort of time but uncle she say to i thatbe go for ever cheerful along with i retire when other be by fond of go any distance fur to teach a child or fur to tend a sick person or fur to do some kindness tow rd a young girl s wedding and shebe do a many but have never see one fondly love of her uncle patient like by young and old sowt out by all that have any trouble
thatbe em ly he draw his hand across his face and with a half-suppress sigh look up from the fire
be martha with you yet i ask
martha he reply get marry mas r davy in the second year
a young man a farm-labourer as come by we on his way to market with his mas r s dray a journey of over five hundred mile theer and back make offer fur to take her fur his wife wife be very scarce theer and then to set up fur their two self in the bush

why someun even make offer fur to marry she

peggotty burst into a great roar of laughter and agnes and i both keep he company

mr
peggotty point to a certain paragraph in the newspaper where i read aloud as follow from the port middlebay times the public dinner to our distinguished fellow-colonist and townsman wilkins micawber esquire port middlebay district magistrate come off yesterday in the large room of the hotel which be crowd to suffocation
it be estimate that not few than forty-seven person must have be accommodate with dinner at one time exclusive of the company in the passage and on the stair
the beauty fashion and exclusiveness of port middlebay flock to do honour to one so deservedly esteemed so highly talente and so widely popular
doctor mell of colonial salem-house grammar school port middlebay preside and on his right sit the distinguished guest
after the removal of the cloth and the singing of non nobis beautifully execute and in which we be at no loss to distinguish the bell-like note of that gifted amateur wilkins micawber esquire junior the usual loyal and patriotic toast be severally give and rapturously receive
doctor mell in a speech replete with feeling then propose our distinguish guest the ornament of our town
may he never leave we but to well himself and may his success among we be such as to render his better himself impossible the cheer with which the toast be receive defy description
again and again it rise and fall like the wave of ocean
at length all be hush and wilkins micawber esquire present himself to return thank
far be it from we in the present comparatively imperfect state of the resource of our establishment to endeavour to follow our distinguished townsman through the smoothly-flow period of his polished and highly-ornate address
suffice it to observe that it be a masterpiece of eloquence and that those passage in which he more particularly trace his own successful career to its source and warn the young portion of his auditory from the shoal of ever incur pecuniary liability which they be unable to liquidate bring a tear into the manly eye present
the remain toast be doctor mell mrs
micawber who gracefully bow her acknowledgement from the side-door where a galaxy of beauty be elevate on chair at once to witness and adorn the gratifying scene mrs
ridger beg late miss micawber mrs
mell wilkins micawber esquire junior who convulse the assembly by humorously remark that he find himself unable to return thank in a speech but would do so with their permission in a song mrs
micawber s family well know it be needless to remark in the mother-country c

peggotty pointing to another part of the paper my eye rest on my own name and i read thus to david copperfield esquire the eminent author
my dear sir year have elapse since i have an opportunity of ocularly peruse the lineament now familiar to the imagination of a considerable portion of the civilized world
but my dear sir though estrange by the force of circumstance over which i have have no control from the personal society of the friend and companion of my youth i have not be unmindful of his soar flight
nor have i be debar though sea between we braid ha roar burns from participate in the intellectual feast he have spread before we
i cannot therefore allow of the departure from this place of an individual whom we mutually respect and esteem without my dear sir take this public opportunity of thank you on my own behalf and i may undertake to add on that of the whole of the inhabitants of port middlebay for the gratification of which you be the minister agent
go on my dear sir
you be not unknown here you be not unappreciated
though remote we be neither unfriended melancholy nor i may add slow
go on my dear sir in your eagle course
the inhabitant of port middlebay may at least aspire to watch it with delight with entertainment with instruction
among the eye elevate towards you from this portion of the globe will ever be find while it have light and life the eye appertain to wilkins micawber magistrate i find on glance at the remain content of the newspaper that mr
micawber be a diligent and esteemed correspondent of that journal

for em ly he say as he put it in his breast

i see myself with agnes at my side journey along the road of life

what face be the most distinct to i in the fleeting crowd
lo these all turn to i as i ask my thought the question
here be my aunt in strong spectacle an old woman of four-score year and more but upright yet and a steady walker of six mile at a stretch in winter weather
always with she here come peggotty my good old nurse likewise in spectacle accustom to do needle-work at night very close to the lamp but never sit down to it without a bit of wax candle a yard-measure in a little house and a work-box with a picture of st
paul s upon the lid
the cheek and arm of peggotty so hard and red in my childish day when i wonder why the bird donot peck she in preference to apple be shrivel now and her eye that use to darken their whole neighbourhood in her face be fainter though they glitter still but her rough forefinger which i once associate with a pocket nutmeg-grater be just the same and when i see my least child catch at it as it totter from my aunt to she i think of our little parlour at home when i could scarcely walk
my aunt s old disappointment be set right now
she be godmother to a real living betsey trotwood and dora the next in order say she spoil she
there be something bulky in peggotty s pocket
it be nothing small than the crocodile book which be in rather a dilapidate condition by this time with diver of the leaf tear and stitch across but which peggotty exhibit to the child as a precious relic
i find it very curious to see my own infant face look up at i from the crocodile story and to be remind by it of my old acquaintance brooks of sheffield

she be in a garden and near she stand a sharp dark withered woman with a white scar on her lip

thus i leave they thus i always find they thus they wear their time away from year to year
what ship come sail home from india and what english lady be this marry to a growling old scotch croesus with great flap of ear
can this be julia mills
indeed it be julia mills peevish and fine with a black man to carry card and letter to she on a golden salver and a copper-colour woman in linen with a bright handkerchief round her head to serve her tiffin in her dressing-room
but julia keep no diary in these day never sing affection s dirge eternally quarrel with the old scotch croesus who be a sort of yellow bear with a tanned hide
julia be steep in money to the throat and talk and think of nothing else
i like she well in the desert of sahara
or perhaps this be the desert of sahara
for though julia have a stately house and mighty company and sumptuous dinner every day i see no green growth near she nothing that can ever come to fruit or flower
what julia call society i see among it mr
jack maldon from his patent place sneer at the hand that give it he and speak to i of the doctor as so charmingly antique
but when society be the name for such hollow gentleman and lady julia and when its breeding be profess indifference to everything that can advance or can retard mankind i think we must have lose ourselves in that same desert of sahara and have well find the way out
and lo the doctor always our good friend labour at his dictionary somewhere about the letter d and happy in his home and wife
also the old soldier on a considerably reduce footing and by no mean so influential as in day of yore
work at his chamber in the temple with a busy aspect and his hair where he be not bald make more rebellious than ever by the constant friction of his lawyer s-wig i come in a later time upon my dear old traddle
his table be cover with thick pile of paper and i say as i look around i if sophy be your clerk now traddles she would have enough to do you may say that my dear copperfield
but those be capital day too in holborn court
be they not when she tell you you would be a judge
but it be not the town talk then at all event say traddle if i ever be one why you know you will be well my dear copperfield when i be one i shall tell the story as i say i would we walk away arm in arm
i be go to have a family dinner with traddle
it be sophy s birthday and on our road traddle discourse to i of the good fortune he have enjoy
i really have be able my dear copperfield to do all that i have most at heart
therebe the reverend horace promote to that living at four hundred and fifty pound a year there be our two boy receive the very good education and distinguish themselves as steady scholar and good fellow there be three of the girl marry very comfortably there be three more live with we there be three more keep house for the reverend horace since mrs

here establish in perpetuity be the poor beauty a widow with a little girl here at dinner on sophy s birthday be the three married girl with their three husband and one of the husband s brother and another husband s cousin and another husband s sister who appear to i to be engage to the cousin
